title: Greek Summer Reaches a Peak
content:
On the edge of the Vikos Gorge, you can peer thousands of feet down at a thick canopy of pine trees that plunges toward a river. According to the Guinness World Records, it’s the deepest canyon relative to its width. Although Greece is 80% mountainous, tourists rarely associate the country with such alpine landscapes. The forested region of Epirus, sprawling along the Albanian border, is the exact opposite of Greece’s famous sun-drenched islands. The mountains are dotted with stone villages where the main source of traffic is shepherds guiding their flocks.

On a typical summer day, it can be significantly cooler here than on the crowded islands. Furthermore, the highest-end hotels cost a fraction of the exorbitant rates that coastal resorts command in August. Greece recently welcomed a record number of international visitors, but most concentrate on tried-and-true spots like Athens and Santorini. Epirus attracts virtually none of that, accounting for less than two percent of overnight stays in the country. The Greek government has noted both this disparity and the area’s unique appeal.

Consequently, significant funds have been committed to infrastructure upgrades and nature-based tourism initiatives in the region. The objective is to encourage travel beyond the crowded islands and toward these mountainous communities that need the economic boost. As global tourism sets records year after year, travelers have realized that everyone else is flocking to the same spots. The new goal is to escape the crowds without sacrificing familiar comforts, bringing secondary and tertiary destinations into the spotlight. Additionally, the Mediterranean’s increasingly punishing summer heat adds further incentive to seek higher altitudes.
